coredata.dc:title: Laser radiation, CDs and Pd-NPs: Three influential factors to enhance the hydrogen storage capacity of porous silicon
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kohzadi, Kosar
Mehrabi, Mohsen
shirkani, Hossein
Karimi, Sadegh
coredata.dc:description: In This study, n-type single-crystal silicon was used to make porous silicon by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods to investigate the hydrogen storage capacity. Carbon dots are made via a hydrothermal method, and palladium nanoparticles are decorated on porous silicon by electroless and drip processes. The morphology of the structures has been investigated using field emission scanning electron microscope, atomic force microscope and X-ray diffraction. The samples' electrochemical characteristics and hydrogen storage capacity have been studied by cyclic voltammetry and chronopotentiometry methods. The results show that laser irradiation for 0.5 h on the back of silicon wafers during the etching prosses increases the storage capacity in porous silicon by 45%. Adding carbon dots and palladium nanoparticles to porous silicon made by the photoelectrochemical method increases hydrogen storage capacity by 29% and 64%, respectively. The hydrogen storage capacity in porous silicon made by the photoelectrochemical method simultaneously decorated with palladium nanoparticles and carbon dots was 1.02% by weight, which shows an increase of 82% compared to undecorated porous silicon.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical
Photoelectrochemical
Porous silicon
Carbon dots
Palladium nanoparticles
Hydrogen storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PS by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical method

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the Persian Gulf University Research Council. We thank Ms. Angham Hosseini (SAMA Research Center) for her help in facilitating the electrochemical studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of the formation of PS by a) electrochemical and b) photoelectrochemical methods.
Steps of synthesis and layering of Pd-NPs on PS by electroless method.
TEM image of CDs made by hydrothermal method.
X-ray diffraction pattern a) carbon dot synthesized by hydrothermal method b) silicon wafer c) PS made with 100 mA current and laser radiation for 30 min decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs.
Silicon wafer a) AFM image b) Surface roughness curve.
PS formed with a current of 100 mA for 30 min a) AFM images b) Surface roughness curve.
AFM images of porous silicon formed with a current of 100 mA for 30 min a) 10 min laser irradiation b) 20 min laser irradiation c)30 min laser irradiation d) Surface roughness curve of sample c.
PS formed with a current of 100 mA for 30 min and 30 min laser irradiation decorated via pd-nanoparticles and CDs a) AFM images b) Surface roughness curve.
CV diagram of PS made by electrochemical, photoelectrochemical methods and decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs.
Adsorption capacity diagram of PS samples made by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods and decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs.
FESEM images for PS sample created by photoelectrochemical method and decorated with Pd-NPs and carbon dot.
Porosity percentage and hydrogen storage capasity in different samples.

row.entry: PS (photoelectrochemical, 30 min laser irradiation + Pd- NPs)
PS (photoelectrochemical, 30 min laser irradiation + CDs + Pd-NPs)

head.ce:title: Laser radiation, CDs and Pd-NPs: Three influential factors to enhance the hydrogen storage capacity of porous silicon

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Persian Gulf University, Bushehr, Iran, P. O. Box: 7516913817
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Persian Gulf University, Bushehr, Iran, P. O. Box: 7516913817

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Persian Gulf University, Bushehr, Iran, P. O. Box: 7516913817
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Persian Gulf University, Bushehr, Iran, P. O. Box: 7516913817

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In This study, n-type single-crystal silicon was used to make porous silicon by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods to investigate the hydrogen storage capacity. Carbon dots are made via a hydrothermal method, and palladium nanoparticles are decorated on porous silicon by electroless and drip processes. The morphology of the structures has been investigated using field emission scanning electron microscope, atomic force microscope and X-ray diffraction. The samples' electrochemical characteristics and hydrogen storage capacity have been studied by cyclic voltammetry and chronopotentiometry methods. The results show that laser irradiation for 0.5 h on the back of silicon wafers during the etching prosses increases the storage capacity in porous silicon by 45%. Adding carbon dots and palladium nanoparticles to porous silicon made by the photoelectrochemical method increases hydrogen storage capacity by 29% and 64%, respectively. The hydrogen storage capacity in porous silicon made by the photoelectrochemical method simultaneously decorated with palladium nanoparticles and carbon dots was 1.02% by weight, which shows an increase of 82% compared to undecorated porous silicon.

ce:section.ce:para: The non-renewability of fossil fuels and their degradable effects on the environment has caused attention to be drawn to green and renewable energy sources. Hydrogen, one of the most abundant elements around humans, is a suitable candidate for use in fuel cells due to its high energy potential in this field. Therefore, it can be an ideal energy to replace fossil fuels. In this way, how to use this energy safely, as well as specific resources for its maintenance and storage methods with very high security and affordable cost, are the most critical criteria in applying hydrogen as a fuel [1]. Despite several scientific research, a safe, effective, and cheap method for hydrogen storage remains a significant challenge to date [2]. Therefore, substantial research has been done on different storage methods and materials on these systems. One of the extensive studies is the improvement of materials in the electrochemical prosses to obtain the highest storage capacity and also reversible, while its characteristics are resereved [3]. The materials' physical, chemical, and biological properties on the nanoscale differ from their bulk properties. These unique properties of nanostructured materials lead to new fields in different branches of science.

Moreover, nanostructures have been very interesting in the past years due to their unique properties in mechanical, electrical, electronic, optical, magnetic, and surface fields [4–7]. While being a very suitable substrate for energy storage, the high surface-to-volume ratio of these materials also has significant consequences. The main features of hydrogen storage devices are increased surface area and the possibility of adding nanomaterials to each other. Nanostructured systems such as boron nitride nanotubes, nano magnesium-based hydrides, hydride/carbon complex nanocomposites, carbon nanotubes, polymer nanocomposites, and organometallic frameworks are considered potential options for hydrogen storage in very high amounts [8–11]. For example, carbon nanotubes have been widely investigated for hydrogen storage by electrochemical methods, and this capacity value for single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes varies from 0.2 % wt to 3.7 % wt [12].

Due to its unique properties, such as high porosity and high specific surface, one of the materials that have been studied. Its capacities and interaction with hydrogen have been investigated in porous silicon (PS) and PS nanoparticles. In various researches, Lysenko et al. and Swihart et al. showed that the hydrogen reaction and coating of porous silicon nanostructures significantly depend on the shape and size of the PS nanostructures. The results of their investigations clearly state that when the interaction of porous silicon nanostructures with fluids occurs, the fractal characteristic of the specific surface should be considered [13,14]. In another study, Mussabek et al. investigated the kinetics of hydrogen production from the oxidation of hydrogenated silicon nanocrystals obtained by mechanical grinding of porous silicon (PSi) layers. Their results show that the hydrogen generation rate depends on chemical composition, the concentration of alkali added in the oxidizing solutions, and the porous silicon nano-powder size distribution [15]. PS is made by creating nano-sized pores on the silicon wafer. In this regard 2009, Manilow et al. [16], using PS and palladium nanoparticles (Pd-NPs), achieved that the Ps surface is capable of good hydrogen storage due to its unique characteristics. Of course, by adding a catalyst to this level, the amount of this storage will be much higher [17]. Also, in 2019, Merazga et al. made nanoporous silicon layers and used them for hydrogen storage. They investigated the effect of PS thickness, porosity, and surface area on hydrogen storage by infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), cyclic voltammetry (CV), contact angle, and capacitive voltage measurements. Hydrogen storage has also been carried out utilizing a three-electrode cell and 3 M sulfuric acid as an electrolyte using cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic charge/discharge methods. Finally, they achieved the highest value for storing hydrogen in their silicon wafers, 86 mAH/g [18]. Various materials have been introduced and examined in this field. For example, since Iijima announced the synthesis of CNTs in 1991 [19], this material could open a suitable place, among others, as potential hydrogen storage. The unique features of this material have proven that they can easily store hydrogen by electrochemical methods [20]. Since graphene and carbon substrates have been sufficiently investigated in this field, and favourable results have been achieved, designing a composite with much higher hydrogen storage capacity is much desired. A material that, in addition to being added to the previously obtained system, is also decorated with catalytic impurities and increases the storage capacity. According to the investigations carried out in this field, graphene is one of the other materials with a nanostructure for use in the hydrogen storage process due to its characteristics such as high surface area, cheapness, and chemically ineffective. In 2005, Sergio et al., almost at the beginning of the hydrogen storage path, partially brought the storage results closer to the global goals by ignoring the quantum effects in the theoretical research before them [21]. Also, in the following years, due to the great importance of this topic in the world of science, as well as carbon derivatives such as graphene quantum dots, which are leaders in this field and have a high potential for this purpose, Martin Pomera collected different information in the direction of energy storage in various ways on its substrate [22]. In addition to the mentioned cases, studies show that some transition metals, such as titanium (Ti), Pd, and vanadium (Va), efficiently react with hydrogen, and the conditions for these reactions are high-speed and convenient at ambient temperatures and pressures. Therefore, such cases cause elements of transition metals to be considered efficient catalysts for hydrogen reactions [23]. Among these, Pd as a catalyst is suitable for hydrogen adsorption and spillover. Therefore, this element is loaded on the surface of various materials to increase the storage properties of this material. For example 2017, Hamid Ghorbani et al. made a PS substrate using the electrochemical anodization method and decorated it with palladium nanoparticles (Pd-NPs) using an electroless process.

Furthermore, their triple sample consisting of silicon substrate carbon nanotubes and Pd-NPs was obtained, and they could observe the maximum hydrogen storage capacity of 537 mAh/g, which is about 2.05% by weight [24]. In 2012, Vinayan Bhagavati et al. studied graphene, nitrogen-doped graphene, and Pd-decorated nitrogen-doped graphene nanocomposites and obtained a better response than the previous sample in hydrogen adsorption and storage, respectively. They concluded that the bond between the catalyst support and the catalyst particles provides a higher hydrogen adsorption capacity [25]. There are different ways to measure hydrogen storage capacity in materials, such as volumetric [8], electrochemical [26], etc. The electrochemical method is the best candidate for testing due to hydrogen storage in austere conditions, with ambient pressure and temperature [27], easy operation, being in hand, and high accuracy. Much work has been done in this field for a long time.

For example, in 1997, Visintin et al. performed charge and discharge on Pd-coated base metal alloys for use in nickel-metal hydride batteries and reported that the effect of Pd coating on The electrochemical storage characteristics was very evident and showed that the alloys coated with Pd exhibited a higher storage capacity than the alloys without Pd coating [28]. In 1999, Bigavo et al. made single-walled carbon nanotubes by laser ablation method. They combined them electrochemically with lithium, and before processing the nanotubes with a ball mill, storage capacities of 600 mAh/g were obtained. After processing with a ball mill, the capacity has increased to 1000 mAh/g [29]. According to the previous reports on the materials' storage properties, we decided to prepare a composite with more extensive research, which, while fresh, will take a new step among the materials made until today. Our unique hybrid of Ps is made by the anodization method and laser light irradiation and is also decorated with Pd-NPs. Next, Carbon dots (CDs) are added as a layer on PS/Pd. The samples were analyzed by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) analysis, and their hydrogen storage capacity was investigated by electrochemical method.

In this study, precursors of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), ethanol (C₂H₆O), hydrofluoric acid (HF), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), palladium chloride (PdCl2) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were prepared by Merck company with high purity. n-type silicon wafer with a resistance of 1–10 Ohm/cm, doped with phosphorus and crystalline direction (100) with a thickness of 500 μm has been used during the experiment. The morphology and structural properties of the synthesized sample were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) Philips EM 208S, Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) ZEISS Sigma 300 model and atomic force microscope (AFM) model BRISK, ARA Research, Iran has taken. The structure of the samples was determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) model D8 Advance, BRUKER, Germany, with a scanning angle from 10 to 80°.

Electrochemical characteristics were performed by a three-electrode cell in which the working electrodes were silicon wafer, PS, PS/Pd, PS/CDs and PS/Pd/CDs, respectively. The platinum plate was chosen as the counter electrode, and the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. In order to minimize the ohmic drop of the electrolyte, the reference electrode was placed near the surface of the working electrode. During this process, the working electrode's potential was checked against the reference electrode. CV and CHP measurements were performed using a potentiostat/galvanostat (Sama500) in a three-electrode glass cell. Galvanostatic measurements for charge/discharge performance were performed under the condition of 10 mA current density for 1 h, and after 5 min rest, a current density of −10 mA was applied for discharge. Potential measurements were carried out in a 3.0 M H2SO4 electrolyte. CV measurements were performed at a variable closed-circuit potential of −0.7 to 0.4 V with a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PS by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical method
ce:section.ce:para: PS was obtained by electrochemical anodization of an n-type single crystal Si wafer with (100) direction, resistance 0–10 Ohm/cm and thickness 500 μm. The silicon wafer was cut in 1 × 1 cm dimensions and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min to remove any contamination and impurities from its surface. Then it was floated with 5% hydrofluoric acid diluted with water with a volume ratio of 1:20 for about 30 s to remove the surface oxide. Then it was directly placed in a mixture of HF (48%), and pure ethanol (99.9%). With a volume ratio of 1:1. Then, anodization was performed with a variable current density of 100 mA for 30 min. In the anodization cell, the platinum plate is connected to the negative pole and the silicon wafer is connected to the positive pole of the power supply, and they are placed in front of each other with a distance of 1 cm. Finally, the sample was washed with distilled water and ethanol to remove HF from the PS surface and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 15 min. For the synthesis of PS by photoelectrochemical method, the previous step was carefully repeated with the difference that at the same time as the current was applied, diode laser light with a wavelength of 850 nm and constant power of 500 mW was irradiated to the silicon wafer from the back at different times of 10, 20 and 30 min, the thickness of the silicon wafer has been measured after cutting it to 1 ∗ 1 cm (m1), after electrochemical etching (m2) and after dissolving and removing the silicon layer (m3) [30,31] (see Fig. 1).

Pd nanoparticles were made by the electroless method immediately after forming the porous layer. At room temperature, 0.008 g of PdCl2 was dissolved in 50 ml of distilled water for 15 min on a magnetic stirrer. Put 50 ml of 0.04 M HCl solution with PdCl2 solution on a magnetic stirrer for 24 h until thoroughly mixed and got uniform. Right after exiting the HF solution, the PS from the previous step was submerged in the aforementioned solution for 3 min. Then the samples were washed with DI water and dried under nitrogen gas. Fig. 2
shows the synthesis steps of Pd-NPs decorated on PS by electrolysis.

At room temperature, 0.1 g of glucose powder was dissolved in 150 ml of distilled water for 15 min on a magnetic stirrer. A one-step hydrothermal method was used to perform the reaction by transferring the colorless liquid solution into a 1-L stainless-steel autoclave and heating it to 473 K for 8 h. The solution turned dark brown after the reaction was prepared and completed. The final solution was passed through filter paper twice. The solution was centrifuged thrice at 14000 rpm for 10 min to isolate small-sized particles from large-sized ones.

To add CDs to the Ps substrate decorated with Pd-NPs, 5 ml of CDs solution synthesized by the mentioned method was poured dropwise on the substrate. After pouring each drop, The substrate surface was nitrogen-dried after each drop was poured.

Fig. 3 shows the TEM images of CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method. As it is clear from the figure, the CDs are well formed with a diameter of less than 50 nm, spherical structure and uniform size distribution.

Figures (4 a&c) show the X-ray diffraction analysis for CDs, silicon wafers, and PS decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs. According to Fig. 4a, the broad peak located at 2θ = 5.22° is assigned to the (002) plane corresponding to the 0444-75 JCPDS card number. This broad peak can be attributed to the amorphous structure of carbon [31]. In Fig. 4b, the crystal structure of the silicon wafer is determined by XRD. On the other hand, in Fig. 4b, the characteristic diffraction peak of a silicon wafer thin film (2θ = 69.5°) corresponding to the (0 0 1) plane verifies the single crystal structure of the high-purity silicon wafer [30,31]. Finally, the structural characteristics of the PS substrate decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs were determined(Fig. 4c legend) from the representative peaks at 2θ = 30.40°, 46.83° related to the planes (1 1 1) and (0 0 2) along with a sharp peak of the silicon wafer (card number 01–1201). So that all the obtained results from nanocomposite XRD analysis confirmed the structure of the silicon wafer, CDs, and Pd-NPs [31–34].

The surface morphology of the samples has been investigated using atomic force microscope analysis. The surface images and roughness diagram for different samples are given in Figs. 5–8
. Fig. 5 shows the flat and smooth surface of a silicon wafer with no porosity, therefore no pores can be seen.

The surface morphology using AFM analysis of the PS sample made with a current of 100 mA for 30 min can be seen in Fig. 6. Based on the obtained results, it has been confirmed that the Etching mechanism has created more porosity, resulting in uniformly distributed pores with a very high density. This has led to an increase in hydrogen storage capacity.

In addition, the surface morphology of the PS layers produced through the photoelectrochemical method was examined and compared with the samples prepared using the electrochemical method. Generally, Fig. 7 illustrates the results of using a 100 mA current for 30 min to produce PS, followed by laser radiation for 10, 20, and 30 min. According to the analysis, silicon's porosity increases as laser irradiation's duration increases. Moreover, In Fig. 7 c, the distribution of dense pores can be seen in the PS created with a current of 100 mA for 30 min and laser light with a power of 500 mW for 10 min. This confirms creating more porosity in these samples made by the photoelectrochemical method and increasing its storage capacity.

The surface morphology of PS layers made via photoelectrochemical method with 100 mA current for 30 min and laser irradiation, which decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs, using AFM analysis and for comparison with samples, has already been investigated. Fig. 8 shows the AFM image related to this sample along with the surface roughness diagram of the analysis related to the PS sample contaminated with nanoparticles, which shows the surface roughness to a lesser extent than the sample without this nanoparticle contamination, and shows a more uniform and smooth surface. This issue represents the occupation of valleys by nanoparticles.

The cyclic voltammetry, CV, and curves of the prepared samples were depicted in Fig. 9 , in the range of −0.7 to 0.4 V and a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s. For all samples, both the cathodic and anodic peak currents were, attributed to the adsorption and desorption of H2 gas observed during the scan. Furthermore, a small anodic peak appeared at (∼0.3 V), which belongs to the monocrystalline Si sample. In the sample with a current of 100 mA in 30 min, the difference is significantly evident, and the adsorption peak appeared in the region (∼0.11V) and with a higher height than wafer silicon, which indicates more adsorption in this sample. Because the position of the peaks represents the speed of the chemical reaction, moving the peaks to lower potentials means a higher speed in the chemical reaction, which is a more suitable option for hydrogen storage. Therefore, the increase in the height of the adsorption and desorption peak, along with the displacement of this peak in the sample with an applied current of 100 mA for 30 min, confirms that the results obtained for hydrogen storage in this sample are better than the silicon wafer. In the samples made by the photoelectrochemical method, the adsorption and desorption peaks are clearly defined and show a greater intensity than the sample without radiation, which indicates a greater hydrogen storage capacity. However, more precisely and according to the data, it is evident that the adsorption peak height in the 10-min laser irradiation sample (V ∼ -0.52) is lower than in the 20-min laser irradiation formed in (V ∼ -0.52). The most prominent peak is the 30-min laser irradiation obtained at (V ∼ -0.51) at a higher height than the previous samples, indicating a higher adsorption capacity than other interests. Cyclic voltammetry curve of the substrate selected from the previous steps, which is PS created with a current of 100 mA for 30 min and 30 min of laser irradiation with a power of 500 mW, along with contamination of nanoparticles in the range of −0.7 up to 0.1 V and shown in the scan tone of 0.1 mV/s. The obtained data show that the adsorption peak in the sample with the CDs layer is lower than the sample of PS decorated with Pd-NPs, which indicates the more effective role of Pd-NPs than CDs in hydrogen adsorption and spillover. The most significant peak belongs to the PS decorated with Pd-NPs and the CDs layer, indicating its higher adsorption capacity. The adsorption peaks at V ∼ −0.55, V ∼ −0.56, and V ∼ −0.58 V and with a much higher height than undecorated PS have appeared for PS made by photoelectrochemical at 10, 20 and 30 min laser radiation.

Galvanostatic chronopotentiometry electrochemical measurements were used to evaluate the hydrogen storage capacity in the samples. This method initially charges the working electrode to a specific time by the required current density. After that, we switch off the current so the potential relaxes to an equilibrium value. The electrode is discharged afterwards to the same or lower current density until the potential reaches the cut-off value or where the potential does not change, and the curve is fixed, and again current is switched off at this point. So, the storage capacity can be obtained by determining the time that the potential is fixed from the discharging curve and putting it in Eq. (1). During the charging process, the potential rises slowly and continuously and in the discharging process, these changes will be faster. For this purpose, charge/discharge measurements of the electrodes are recorded at a current density of 10 mA. The hydrogen storage capacity (SC) in the charge/discharge curves is calculated from the discharge time using the following equation:
(1) SC = t × [(1 h)/(3600 s)] × j × (1)/m
Where j is the discharge current (mA), t is the time (s), and m is the mass of the electrode (g).

Electrochemical-specific capacity is obtained in mAh/g. While the specific capacity of the gas phase is expressed as weight per cent (wt%). This relationship between the gas phase and electrochemical capacity is determined by simple dimensional analysis. Therefore, 1% by weight of gas phase storage is equal to 268 mAh/g of electrochemical capacity. According to this relationship, the capacities can be converted into weight per cent [35]. The discharge curves of the samples are shown in Fig. 10
. The storage capacity of the Si wafer shows 68.57 mAh/g, which is the lowest value compared to other samples.

The data and information of the samples made by electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods and previous related works are fully listed in Table 1 . Porosity is the most crucial parameter quantity to describe porous structures. This relationship is calculated by the following equation and by weighing:
(2) P=(m1-m2/m1-m3) ∗ 100

P is the porosity percentage, m
1 is the initial wafer mass, m
2 is the wafer mass after electrochemical etching, and m
3 is the silicon mass after dissolving and removing its porous layer [36].

The PS has a higher hydrogen storage capacity than crystalline Si due to its surface roughness, higher porosity percentage, and holes to trap hydrogen molecules and atoms. Although defects and roughness on the surface of Si can create active sites for hydrogen adsorption and storage, their size and number are still small and limited. As a result, we attempted to enhance the storage capacity of this substrate by altering the manufacturing method and the silicon surface. The discharge curve and data presented in Table 1 indicate that PS produced through the electrochemical method exhibits a 90% boost in hydrogen storage capacity compared to the silicon wafer. The sample produced through irradiating laser light with a power of 500 mW for 30 min has the highest hydrogen storage capacity among these three samples. It can hold 150 mAh/g of hydrogen, which is about 1.4 times more than PS made by the electrochemical method and 2.7 times more than silicon wafers. According to previous studies, the sample's surface needs holes (positive charges) to create silicon porosity. In n-type silicon, the Fermi level is located near the conduction band, and during the fabrication of samples, there are upward band bends on the surface. A significant amount of electrons and positive charges are generated within the structure nearby during laser irradiation. Since silicon has an indirect bandgap, the electron vacancies create electrons and holes that do not recombine easily but spread and move continuously. Besides, due to the internal field generated by the bandgap bending, many holes can move toward the back of the sample that a laser has irradiated. These holes are trapped on the surface behind the laser beam and then participate in the formation of PS. This issue explains why the porosity is formed in the opposite part of the laser light and not on the top surface of the silicon. Additionally, the amount of porosity in the samples rises as the duration of laser irradiation increases [37,38]. So that The sample made by the photoelectrochemical method was considered a suitable porous substrate for hydrogen storage. Hence The impact of incorporating CDs and Pd-NPs onto this surface was examined. The storage capacity is enhanced by 29% when CDs are added to the PS substrate (Table 1 and Fig. 10). Also, the addition of Pd-NPs on the substrate has increased this amount to 64%. As it is known, the highest hydrogen storage capacity for the sample contaminated with CDs and Pd-NPs is 272 mAh/g, which is about 1.8 times higher than the hydrogen storage capacity of PS. CDs have an excellent ability to absorb hydrogen, so they act as active sites for hydrogen storage by entering these nanoparticles on the uneven substrate of PS. Therefore, the CDs/PS electrode can be expected to have a higher hydrogen storage capacity than PS. Generally, the following features can explain the excellent hydrogen storage capacity of the GQD/Pd/PS electrode.

(1) Potent catalytic property of Pd nanoparticles and spillover phenomenon; 2) Hydrogen adsorption property on the CDs surface (3) Uniform distribution of Pd nanoparticles and CDs on the surface of PS to facilitate charge transfer from the electrode to the interface [10,12,15,16]. The FESM images of the PS substrate produced by the photoelectrochemical method and decorated with Pd-NPs and CDs are shown in Fig. 11. The images clearly show the holes and the uniform distribution of nanoparticles on the substrate, which support the reasons for enhancing the hydrogen storage capacity.

Hydrogen energy is one of the very clean types of energy with high efficiency. The main obstacle to using it is its storage because of the danger of explosion. As mentioned in previous researches, in the electrochemical etching method for preparing porous silicon, by covering the dangling bonds of Si with hydrogen on the obtained surface, silane groups SiHx (x = 1, 2, 3) are formed. In pure silicon with (111) direction, single Si–H species often cover the crystal planes. Therefore, the physical nature of hydrogen adsorption processes on the primary chemisorbed H monolayer covering the pore walls explains hydrogen adsorption in porous silicon. In short, hydrogen storage with PS is a chemical hydrogen storage method and is mostly done by physical absorption. According to the above-mentioned, PS is a very good candidate for hydrogen storage, and investigations on the improvement of hydrogen storage in it are to be continued [39–41].

In this study, PS is made via two electrochemical and photoelectrochemical methods to investigate hydrogen storage capacity. The results show that the samples made with the help of laser radiation have higher porosity and show more storage capacity, which shows that It can be attributed to the effect of laser radiation on the movement of electrons and the increase of holes with a positive charge and the direction of these positive charges to the surface of the sample and trapping them to increase the reaction with F- ions and as a result increase the corrosion of silicon and create more and deeper holes. Therefore, laser radiation is a practical and helpful factor in making PS increase hydrogen storage capacity. The hydrogen storage capacity in samples exposed to laser radiation is 82% higher than in samples without laser radiation. The study examined the impact of carbon dots and Pd-NPs on hydrogen storage capacity in PS, which is produced via the photoelectrochemical method, and found both effective. Also, the carbon structures enhance the storage capacity as they function as absorbers of hydrogen on their surface, thereby increasing the effective surface area for hydrogen storage. Hydrogen storage efficiency is enhanced by Pd-NPs, which act as catalysts for absorption and spillover processes. The studies clearly show that laser radiation during the acid etching process, the addition of CDs, and the addition of Pd-NPs are three influential factors in increasing hydrogen storage capacity in PS. Finally, our investigations confirm that laser radiation, CDs, and Pd-NPs boost hydrogen storage capacity in PS during acid etching.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the Persian Gulf University Research Council. We thank Ms. Angham Hosseini (SAMA Research Center) for her help in facilitating the electrochemical studies.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen engine operation strategies: recent progress, industrialization challenges, and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. W. Zehao Sun, Jichao Hong, Tiezhu Zhang , Binbin Sun , Binbin Yang , Liqun Lu , Lin Li. Hydrogen engine operation strategies: Recent progress, industrialization challenges, and perspectives Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, 2023; 48: 366-392 doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2022.09.256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Safety of hydrogen storage and transportation: an overview on mechanisms, techniques, and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. B. Hao Li , Xuewen Cao , Yang Liu , Yanbo Shao , Zilong Nan , Lin Teng , Wenshan Peng. Safety of hydrogen storage and transportation: An overview on mechanisms, techniques, and challenges Energy Reports, 2022; 8: 6258-6269 doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egyr.2022.04.067.
